Figure 1:OPRD is strictly Pareto-dominant on accuracy, training time, and GPU memory.Each bubble is a method trained from the same R1-distill-1.5B student against JustRL-1.5B teacher for500500optimizer steps on8×8\!\times\!A100 GPU (80G) FSDP (§4).
Axes carry the two “compute” costs (wall-clock↓\downarrow, AIME24 Avg@16↑\uparrow); bubble area encodes the third cost (actor-updateΔ\Deltapeak GPU memory↓\downarrow).
OPRD (navy bubble) simultaneously dominates the strongest output-space baseline (OPD top-1616) on all three axes:2.72.7pt accuracy gain,1.44×1.44\timesspeed-up, and54%54\%Δ\Deltapeak memory cut. Results on AIME25 and AIMO are qualitatively identical (§4.2).

Figure 2:Architecture of OPRD vs. output-space OPD.Both methods share the same on-policy rollouty^∼πθ(⋅∣x)\hat{y}\sim\pi_{\theta}(\cdot\!\mid\!x), which is fed to the student (blue, trainable) and the teacher (orange, frozen).OPDextracts supervisionafterthe LM head, comparing output distributionsptp_{t}andqtq_{t}via reverse KL on a token subset.OPRD (ours)extracts supervisionbeforethe LM head, comparing intermediate hidden statesh(l)h^{(l)}at selected layers via maskedℓ2\ell_{2}loss.
OPRD is used on its own by default, but can optionally be combined withℒOPD\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{OPD}}asℒOPD+μ​ℒOPRD\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{OPD}}+\mu\,\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{OPRD}}.
OPRD taps the teacherbeforethe LM-head projection, exposing per-layer structural information that any output-space objective discards.

Figure 4:OPRD produces shorter responses than OPD at higher accuracy.Mean rollout lengthresponse_length/meanalong training for OPRD vs. OPD top-11vs. OPD top-1616(smoothing window=15=15).
OPRD converges to∼5,700{\sim}5{,}700tokens per response, while both OPD variants plateau around∼7,000{\sim}7{,}000tokens, indicating that hidden-state supervision yields more concise and efficient reasoning chains.

Figure 5:OPRD monotonically increases the student–teacher representation cosine similarity it supervises (higher is better).rep/cosine_similarityon the OPRD-supervised positions along training (smoothing window=5=5).
The curve rises sharply early and drifts upward steadily thereafter, confirming that (6) is being optimised end-to-end.

Figure 6:Adding OPRD on top of OPD top-1 monotonically lifts accuracy.AIME24 avg@16 ofℒOPD+μ⋅ℒOPRD\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{OPD}}+\mu\cdot\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{OPRD}}forμ∈{0,1,10}\mu\!\in\!\{0,1,10\}. Evenμ=1\mu\!=\!1already surpasses OPD top-1616(47.147.1);μ=10\mu\!=\!10closes the gap to teacher to within0.60.6pt.

Figure 7:The student diverges from the teacher mostly at the end of the response.Cosine similarity between student (R1-distill-1.5B) and teacher (JustRL-1.5B) last-layer hidden states on on-policy rollouts, restricted to either the firstkkor the lastkkresponse tokens, as a function ofkk(log scale; “ALL” = full response, at which both curves coincide at95.42%95.42\%by construction).
The first-kkcurve is nearly teacher-aligned at everykk(≥97%\geq\!97\%fork≤1600k\!\leq\!1600); the last-kkcurve lags by≥4\geq\!4points untilkkexceeds the full response length.
This empirically motivates concentrating OPRD’s supervision on the last-kkpositions (§4.1).

Figure 8:OPRD accelerates the PG-loss phase transition and validates the information bottleneck.actor/pg_lossalong training for OPD top-11++OPRD composite runs (ℒOPD​top​-​1+μ⋅ℒOPRD\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{OPD\,top\text{-}1}}+\mu\cdot\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{OPRD}},μ∈{0,1,10}\mu\!\in\!\{0,1,10\}; smoothing window=15=15).
All runs show a loss spike (possible phase transition); OPRD shifts it earlier, indicating accelerated distillation.
In late training all curves converge to≈0{\approx}\,0, yet accuracy differences persist, corroborating the LM-head bottleneck ofTheorem˜2.

Figure 9:Adding OPRD to OPD top-1616further aligns student and teacher next-token top-1616sets (higher is better).Validationval-topk/overlap_ratioalong training.
The two runs are nearly co-located early on, but in the second half of training OPD top-1616plateaus while OPD top-1616++OPRD keeps climbing, the same late-stage divergence that distinguishes the accuracy curves ofFigure˜3.
